stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[c]] - \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \right\} \). The terms increase with \( n \) and are bounded above by \( [[c]] \), so by the completeness (least-upper-bound) property of \( \mathbb{R} \), \( \sup S \) exists (even though it is never attained by any term). Find \( \sup S \).
solution: As \( n \to \infty \), \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} \to 0 \), so the increasing bounded sequence approaches its least upper bound: \( \sup S = \lim_{n\to\infty}\left([[c]] - \dfrac{[[d]]}{n}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the first term \( n=1 \), which is the infimum of this increasing set, not the supremum.
This adds instead of subtracts the offset.
This reports only the offset \( d \), not the limiting value.
This divides the two constants for no valid reason.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[c]] - \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \right\} \). Since \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} \) strictly decreases as \( n \) increases, the terms of \( S \) strictly increase, so the smallest term occurs at \( n = 1 \) and equals \( \inf S \) (here the infimum IS attained). Find \( \inf S \).
solution: The set increases with \( n \), so its minimum (attained) value is the \( n=1 \) term: \( \inf S = [[c]] - \dfrac{[[d]]}{1} = [[c]] - [[d]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the limiting value \( \sup S \), not the infimum.
This adds instead of subtracts the offset.
This has the two terms in the wrong order.
This drops the constant \( c \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct infimum.
This is off by one from the correct infimum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[c]] + \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \right\} \). The terms decrease as \( n \) increases and are bounded below by \( [[c]] \), so by the completeness property of \( \mathbb{R} \), \( \inf S \) exists (even though it is never attained by any term). Find \( \inf S \).
solution: As \( n \to \infty \), \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} \to 0 \), so the decreasing bounded-below sequence approaches its greatest lower bound: \( \inf S = \lim_{n\to\infty}\left([[c]] + \dfrac{[[d]]}{n}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the first term \( n=1 \), which is the supremum of this decreasing set, not the infimum.
This subtracts instead of adds the offset.
This reports only the offset \( d \), not the limiting value.
This has the wrong sign on \( c \).
This is off by one from the correct infimum.
This is off by one from the correct infimum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[c]] + \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \right\} \). Since \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{n} \) strictly decreases as \( n \) increases, the terms of \( S \) strictly decrease, so the largest term occurs at \( n = 1 \) and equals \( \sup S \) (here the supremum IS attained). Find \( \sup S \).
solution: The set decreases with \( n \), so its maximum (attained) value is the \( n=1 \) term: \( \sup S = [[c]] + \dfrac{[[d]]}{1} = [[c]] + [[d]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the limiting value \( \inf S \), not the supremum.
This subtracts instead of adds the offset.
This has the two terms in the wrong order.
This drops the constant \( c \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, (-1)^n\dfrac{[[c]]}{n} + [[e]] : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \right\} \). The positive (even-\(n\)) terms \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{2}+[[e]], \dfrac{[[c]]}{4}+[[e]], \dfrac{[[c]]}{6}+[[e]], \dots \) decrease, so the largest one is \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{2}+[[e]] \) at \( n=2 \), which is the maximum, hence the supremum, of the whole set \( S \). Find \( \sup S \).
solution: Among the positive terms (even \( n \)), \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{n} \) is largest at the smallest even \( n=2 \), giving \( \sup S = \dfrac{[[c]]}{2} + [[e]] = [[answer]] \); the negative (odd-\(n\)) terms are all smaller than this.
distractor_rationales: This is the \( n=1 \) term's magnitude plus the shift, not the attained maximum of the set.
This is the next even term \( n=4 \) plus the shift, smaller than the true maximum.
This is the infimum of \( S \) (attained at \( n=1 \)), not the supremum.
This has the wrong sign on the \( c/2 \) part of the correct supremum.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, (-1)^n\dfrac{[[c]]}{n} + [[e]] : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \right\} \). The negative (odd-\(n\)) terms \( -[[c]]+[[e]], -\dfrac{[[c]]}{3}+[[e]], -\dfrac{[[c]]}{5}+[[e]], \dots \) increase toward \( [[e]] \), so the smallest (most negative) one is \( -[[c]]+[[e]] \) at \( n=1 \), which is the minimum, hence the infimum, of the whole set \( S \). Find \( \inf S \).
solution: Among the negative terms (odd \( n \)), \( -\dfrac{[[c]]}{n} \) is smallest (most negative) at the smallest odd \( n=1 \), giving \( \inf S = -[[c]] + [[e]] = [[answer]] \); the positive (even-\(n\)) terms are all larger than this.
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the \( c \) part; the infimum's \( c \)-term here is negative.
This is the next odd term's negative half plus the shift, not the true minimum.
This is the \( n=3 \) term, smaller in magnitude than the true minimum.
This is the supremum of \( S \) (attained at \( n=2 \)), not the infimum.
This is off by one from the correct infimum.
This is off by one from the correct infimum.
